summary: This welder selection chart guides users through choosing the appropriate welding machine and process based on six key decision factors. The chart covers questions about metal thickness, material type, metal thickness ranges, voltage requirements, typical applications, and desired portability. For each decision path, the chart recommends specific welding processes (FLUX-CORED/FCAW, MIG/GMAW, STICK/SMAW, TIG/GTAW) with brief descriptions of their characteristics and ideal use cases. An important safety warning at the top right emphasizes proper power cord selection when using extension cords with welders.
section_hint: Selection Chart / How to Choose a Welder

figures.caption: Six-step welder selection decision tree covering wall outlet voltage, indoor/outdoor use, material type, metal thickness, typical applications, and portability requirements
Welding process comparison showing FLUX-CORED/FCAW, MIG/GMAW, STICK/SMAW, and TIG/GTAW with their key characteristics and applications
Important safety notice about proper power cord and extension cord selection to avoid motor and insulation damage
Duty cycle example showing 10-6-4 rule: 10 min ON, 6 min duty cycle, 4 min OFF

tables.title: Welder Selection Criteria
rows: What type of material are you welding?
• General steel • Light fabrication • Auto body work • Self-shielded & MIG capable
• Steel work • Automobile body • Boat work • Tractor work
• Farm and shop • Service and repair • Ornamental ironwork • Maintenance & repair
• Exhaust pipe repairs • Repair frame • Body sheet metal • Sheet or tubing

tables.title: Welding Process Recommendations
rows: • Ideal for welders or shop/contractors • Produces stronger, solid welds • Flux acts as a shield • Easily welded in windy conditions
• Hot-penetrating thick welding speeds • Stronger welds in many metals • Needs smooth & clean metal • Little post-weld cleanup
• Ideal for service or windy conditions • Produces smoother, solid welds • Flux electrode is self-shielded • Easily welded in windy conditions
• Highest quality welds • Provides control over parameters • Slower than other processes • Little post-weld cleanup
